programs.name: Medicaid for Aged, Blind, Disabled (ABD)
programs.tagline: Medicaid health coverage and long-term care services for adults 65+, blind, or disabled in New Hampshire.
programs.intro:
If your loved one is 65 or older, blind at any age, or disabled, New Hampshire's ABD Medicaid covers medical care and may include home-based services to help them age in place. ABD is actually an umbrella covering multiple programs — from basic health coverage to comprehensive long-term care with case management for bathing, dressing, meals, and housework.

The financial rules are complex and vary by specific ABD program. For long-term care services, your loved one can have up to $7,500 in assets (New Hampshire applies a $5,000 disregard above the base $2,500 limit). Their home is automatically protected if a spouse or disabled child lives there — otherwise, home equity cannot exceed $752,000 in 2026. Income limits differ by program type, and some working disabled adults qualify for higher thresholds.

Because each ABD program has different eligibility rules and benefits, the state recommends applying even if you think income or assets are too high — exceptions and disregards often apply that aren't obvious upfront.

exemptAssets: Primary home (if spouse, child under 21, or disabled child lives there)
Burial funds up to certain limits

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: For long-term care ABD services, must need help with Activities of Daily Living like bathing, dressing, or medication management (clinical assessment required)
otherRequirements: Must be New Hampshire resident
Must be U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through HealthCare.gov or call New Hampshire DHHS at 1-800-852-3345, ext. 9700 — processing time not specified.

steps.title: Identify which ABD program applies
steps.description: Contact DHHS at 1-800-852-3345, ext. 9700 to determine if you need Aid to the Needy Blind, APTD (Aid to the Permanently Disabled), MOAD (working disabled 65+), or Choices for Independence (home care).

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect income records, asset documentation, medical records, and citizenship proof. Contact DHHS for specific document requirements for your ABD program category.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply online at HealthCare.gov, call 1-800-852-3345 ext. 9700, or visit a local DHHS office. You can also mail a paper application (contact DHHS for current mailing address).

steps.title: Complete any required assessments
steps.description: For long-term care ABD programs, a clinical assessment determines if you meet Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements.

steps.title: Plan for annual renewal
steps.description: All ABD Medicaid requires yearly renewal. Plan switching occurs in August if you want to change managed care organizations.

applicationGuide.tip: Apply even if you think your loved one's income or assets are too high — New Hampshire has disregards and exceptions that aren't obvious, and each ABD program has different financial limits.

applicationNotes: Each ABD program (Aid to the Needy Blind, APTD, MOAD, Choices for Independence) has different income limits and benefits — make sure you're applying for the right one
For long-term care ABD, your loved one will pay all monthly income except $93 Personal Needs Allowance toward care costs — this is called Patient Liability
Home equity protection is NOT automatic — it only applies if a spouse, child under 21, or permanently disabled child lives in the home
Working disabled adults (MEAD/MOAD programs) have higher income and asset limits than other ABD categories, but specific amounts vary
relatedPrograms: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)

faqs.question: Can my loved one keep their house if it's worth more than the asset limit?
faqs.answer: Yes, if a spouse, child under 21, or permanently blind/disabled child lives in the home — it's automatically exempt regardless of value. If your loved one lives alone, the home equity cannot exceed $752,000 in 2026. The home's assessed value doesn't matter, only the equity (value minus mortgage debt).

faqs.question: What's the difference between the various ABD programs?
faqs.answer: Aid to the Needy Blind serves blind individuals, APTD covers permanently disabled adults, MOAD helps working disabled adults 65+, and Choices for Independence provides home-based long-term care. Each has different income limits, asset rules, and benefits. Contact DHHS at 1-800-852-3345 ext. 9700 to determine which applies to your situation.

faqs.question: How much of my loved one's income will go toward care costs?
faqs.answer: For long-term care ABD programs, your loved one keeps only $93 per month for personal needs, plus Medicare premiums and potentially a spouse's needs allowance. All other income goes toward care costs — this is called Patient Liability and can be substantial.

faqs.question: Can I apply for multiple ABD programs at the same time?
faqs.answer: You typically apply for the ABD category that fits your situation, not multiple programs simultaneously. However, you may qualify for regular Medicaid and long-term care services together. DHHS will determine all programs you're eligible for during the application process.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's needs change after enrollment?
faqs.answer: ABD Medicaid requires annual renewal, and you must report significant changes in income, assets, or care needs within 10 days. For long-term care programs, reassessments determine if your loved one still meets Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements.

faqs.question: Can a family member apply on behalf of an elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, adult children can apply for their loved ones, but you'll need legal documentation such as power of attorney or guardianship papers. Include these documents with your application to show you're authorized to act on your loved one's behalf.

faqs.question: Should we apply if my loved one has too much income or assets?
faqs.answer: Yes — New Hampshire recommends applying anyway. There are income disregards, asset exemptions, and spend-down options that aren't obvious. For example, the state applies a $5,000 asset disregard above the federal limit, and medical expenses can reduce countable income.

faqs.question: How long does the application process really take?
faqs.answer: Processing times aren't specified in state materials, but applications can be delayed if you're missing required documents or need a disability determination. Submit all documentation at once and follow up if you don't hear back within 45 days.

programs.name: Choices for Independence (CFI) Waiver
programs.tagline: Home care services for parents who qualify medically for nursing home care but want to stay home

localEntities.name: Local DHHS offices, ADRC/ServiceLink centers, NH Care Collaborative, Community Partners

programs.intro:
If your loved one needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management but wants to stay home instead of moving to a nursing home, New Hampshire's Choices for Independence (CFI) Waiver provides care coordination, case management, and home-based services. Your loved one must qualify medically for nursing home placement and meet Medicaid financial guidelines.

Because this is a Medicaid waiver program, one care coordinator manages all services — you won't need to coordinate between multiple providers. Services are tailored to your loved one's assessed needs and may include personal care, home modifications, respite care, and other supports that help them remain safely at home.

summary: Must qualify medically for nursing home care
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet nursing home level of care (a clinical assessment of whether your loved one needs daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other activities of daily living)
otherRequirements: New Hampshire resident
In need of services to live safely at home
Meet Medicaid financial guidelines for long-term care

faqs.question: Can my loved one keep their house if it's worth more than typical Medicaid limits?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary residence is typically exempt from Medicaid asset limits regardless of value, as long as your loved one lives in it or intends to return. However, there may be equity caps — confirm current rules with DHHS at 603-271-9700.

faqs.question: What if my loved one needs nursing home care but the CFI Waiver has no openings?
faqs.answer: Your loved one can receive nursing home care through regular Medicaid while waiting for a CFI Waiver opening. ServiceLink counselors at 1-866-634-9412 can help coordinate interim services and keep your application active.

faqs.question: Can I apply for my loved one if they have dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can apply on behalf of parents who cannot manage their own affairs. You may need power of attorney or guardianship documentation. The DHHS office will guide you through authorized representative procedures.

faqs.question: How do I choose between case management agencies?
faqs.answer: Ask ServiceLink about agencies serving your area, their specialties, and current capacity. Some focus on specific conditions like dementia or have shorter wait times for services.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's needs change after enrollment?
faqs.answer: The care coordinator reassesses needs regularly and can adjust services. If needs increase significantly, they may recommend nursing home placement. If needs decrease, services can be reduced while maintaining waiver enrollment.

faqs.question: Can my loved one have other insurance and still use CFI Waiver services?
faqs.answer: Yes, Medicare and private insurance are used first for covered services. CFI Waiver covers services not paid by other insurance and provides care coordination that ties everything together.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's income is slightly over Medicaid limits?
faqs.answer: New Hampshire may have spend-down options or qualified income trusts for people over income limits. Discuss options with a ServiceLink counselor at 1-866-634-9412 — don't assume your loved one won't qualify.

faqs.question: How quickly can services start after approval?
faqs.answer: Service start times depend on local provider availability and the type of services needed. Personal care may start within days, while home modifications or specialized services may take weeks to arrange.

programs.name: NH PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.tagline: All-inclusive medical and social care under one team — if your loved one needs nursing home-level care but can live safely at home.
programs.intro:
If your loved one is 55+ and needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay at home, PACE provides everything under one roof: primary care, specialists, nursing, therapy, personal care, transportation, meals, medications, and adult day services. Because PACE covers all medical care under one program, your loved one won't need to coordinate between separate providers — one interdisciplinary team manages everything with no deductibles or copays.

New Hampshire does not currently have any operational PACE programs. The state has discussed bringing PACE to NH, but no providers are licensed yet. If your loved one needs comprehensive care coordination now, you'll need to explore other options or consider PACE programs in neighboring states if your loved one could relocate to their service areas.

summary: Can live safely at home with services
Must live in PACE service area
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must require Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment of whether your loved one needs daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other activities that would typically require nursing home placement)
otherRequirements: Must be able to live safely in the community with PACE services
Must live within 30-45 minutes of a PACE center
Currently no PACE centers operate in New Hampshire

steps.title: Explore alternatives

faqs.question: When will PACE actually be available in New Hampshire?
faqs.answer: There's no official timeline. NH has discussed bringing PACE through a state plan amendment, but no providers are licensed yet. Check with NH DHHS at (603) 271-9389 quarterly for updates, as development can take several years once initiated.

faqs.question: Can we apply to a PACE program in Massachusetts or Vermont instead?
faqs.answer: Only if your loved one moves to that state and lives within the PACE center's service area (typically 30-45 minutes drive time). You cannot commute from NH to another state's PACE program — residency in the service area is required.

faqs.question: What's the difference between PACE and regular home care services?
faqs.answer: PACE provides all medical care (doctors, specialists, hospital, prescriptions) plus social services under one team with no copays. Regular home care typically covers personal care only, and you still coordinate separately with doctors, specialists, and pay Medicare/insurance copays.

faqs.question: How do I know if my loved one would qualify for Nursing Facility Level of Care?
faqs.answer: If your loved one needs daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or has cognitive impairment requiring supervision, they may qualify. Contact ServiceLink at (866) 634-9412 for a preliminary assessment — they can evaluate this need for other NH programs.

faqs.question: If my loved one has Medicare and some savings, would PACE cost anything?
faqs.answer: For Medicaid-eligible participants, PACE is free. For Medicare-only participants with income/assets above Medicaid limits, there's a monthly premium that can be substantial. Exact NH rates are unknown since no programs exist yet.

faqs.question: Can my loved one keep their current doctors if they join PACE?
faqs.answer: No. PACE operates as a 'lock-in' program — all care must go through the PACE team except for emergencies. This is why PACE works best for people who need care coordination and are willing to change providers for comprehensive service.

programs.name: Qualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB), Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary (SLMB), Qualified Individual (QI/SLMB-135)
programs.tagline: Cut your loved one's Medicare costs by up to $4,000+ per year if their income is under $1,660/month.
programs.intro:
If your loved one struggles with Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copays, New Hampshire's Medicare Savings Programs can dramatically reduce these costs. Because these programs are tiered by income, your loved one might qualify for full coverage of Medicare costs (QMB tier: saves $4,000+ annually) or just premium help (SLMB/QI tiers: saves $2,435 annually on Part B premiums).

New Hampshire offers a $13 monthly income disregard that many families miss — this means your loved one can earn up to $1,660 monthly as a single person and still qualify for the highest tier. Because these programs automatically trigger Extra Help for prescription drug costs, your loved one gets additional Medicare Part D savings without separate applications.
programs.savingsSource: Part B premiums ($2,435 annually) plus deductibles and copays for QMB tier

otherRequirements: Must be eligible for Medicare Parts A and B (even if not currently enrolled)
New Hampshire residency required

contentSections.title: Three Tiers Based on Income
contentSections.content:
**QMB (up to $1,235 single/$1,663 married)**: Covers ALL Medicare costs — Part A premiums (up to $565/month if applicable), Part A deductible ($1,736 per benefit period), Part A copays, Part B premium ($202.90/month), Part B deductible ($283/year), and Part B 20% coinsurance.

**SLMB ($1,235-$1,478 single/$1,663-$1,992 married)**: Covers Part B premiums only ($202.90/month in 2026).

**QI ($1,478-$1,660 single/$1,992-$2,239 married)**: Covers Part B premiums only, but funding may be limited with first-come priority.

faqs.question: Can my loved one keep their house if it's worth more than the asset limits?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary residence is exempt from the $9,090 individual/$13,630 couple asset limits. Only additional real estate, retirement accounts, and savings count toward the limits.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's income is right at the border between tiers?
faqs.answer: New Hampshire disregards the first $13 of unearned income (like Social Security), which could bump your loved one into a better tier. A single person earning $1,248 in Social Security would qualify for QMB because $1,248 - $13 = $1,235.

faqs.question: Will my loved one automatically get Extra Help for prescriptions?
faqs.answer: Yes. All three Medicare Savings Program tiers automatically qualify your loved one for Extra Help (Low-Income Subsidy) for Medicare Part D prescription costs. No separate application needed.

faqs.question: Can providers still bill my loved one if they have QMB coverage?
faqs.answer: No. Providers cannot bill QMB beneficiaries for any Medicare-covered cost-sharing. If your loved one receives bills for Medicare copays or deductibles, contact the provider immediately to inform them of QMB status.

faqs.question: Is there a waitlist for these programs?
faqs.answer: QMB and SLMB have no waitlists. QI tier may have limited funding with first-come, first-served priority, though New Hampshire-specific waitlist details aren't available.

faqs.question: Can I apply for this and regular Medicaid at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes. Medicare Savings Programs are part of the Medicaid application process through New Hampshire DHHS. Your loved one may qualify for both programs depending on their income and needs.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's income changes after enrollment?
faqs.answer: Report income changes to New Hampshire DHHS immediately. Your loved one may move between tiers or lose eligibility, but they could also qualify for a better tier if income decreases.

faqs.question: Does my loved one need to be enrolled in Medicare Parts A and B to apply?
faqs.answer: Your loved one must be eligible for Medicare Parts A and B but doesn't need to be currently enrolled. The savings program can help pay the premiums once they do enroll.

programs.name: SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program)
programs.tagline: Monthly grocery assistance of $100-400+ for seniors on fixed incomes

localEntities.name: Local DHHS District Offices across NH.

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 60+ and struggling with grocery costs on a fixed income, SNAP provides monthly benefits loaded onto an EBT card to buy food. Because New Hampshire expanded eligibility beyond federal minimums, your loved one may qualify even with income up to $2,608/month for a single person — higher than most states.

Because many seniors don't realize they qualify, only about 40% of eligible elderly participate. Your loved one can use SNAP benefits at grocery stores for most food items, but not hot prepared foods, alcohol, or tobacco. Medical expenses over $35/month count as deductions for seniors, which can significantly boost eligibility.

Because New Hampshire eliminated asset limits for most households, your loved one's savings or home value won't disqualify them unless their income exceeds 200% of federal poverty guidelines and they have more than $4,500 in countable assets.
programs.savingsSource: Monthly EBT benefits based on household size and net income

otherRequirements: U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen
New Hampshire resident
Net income generally at 100% federal poverty level after deductions

faqs.question: Can my loved one get SNAP if they own their home?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your loved one's primary home is completely exempt from asset limits regardless of value. New Hampshire also eliminated asset limits for most households, so home ownership won't affect SNAP eligibility.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's Social Security puts them over the income limit?
faqs.answer: They may still qualify through the net income test. Medical expenses over $35/month and high shelter costs count as deductions for seniors, which can bring net income below the eligibility threshold even if gross income seems too high.

faqs.question: Can I apply for SNAP on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can apply as an authorized representative if your loved one gives permission or if you have legal authority through power of attorney or guardianship. You'll need to provide documentation of your authority to act on their behalf.

faqs.question: How much in SNAP benefits will my loved one actually receive?
faqs.answer: Benefits depend on net income and household size. Generally, every $100 increase in net monthly income reduces SNAP benefits by about $30. A typical elderly single person might receive $200-400/month, while a couple could receive $300-600/month.

faqs.question: What grocery items can my loved one buy with SNAP benefits?
faqs.answer: Most food items including fresh produce, meat, dairy, bread, cereals, and seeds for growing food. SNAP cannot be used for hot prepared foods, alcohol, tobacco, vitamins, household supplies, or pet food.

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply for SNAP and LIHEAP energy assistance at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes, these are separate programs and applying for one doesn't affect the other. Both use NHEasy online system, so you can often apply for multiple benefits in one session.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's income changes after they're approved for SNAP?
faqs.answer: Report income changes to DHHS within 10 days. Benefits will be recalculated based on the new income. If income increases significantly, benefits may be reduced or stopped, but if income drops, benefits may increase.

faqs.question: How quickly can my loved one start using SNAP benefits after approval?
faqs.answer: The EBT card typically arrives by mail within 7-10 days of approval. Benefits are loaded monthly on the same day each month based on the last digit of your loved one's case number.

programs.name: LIHEAP (Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program)
programs.tagline: Get $94 to $2,177 toward your loved one's heating bills if their income is under $3,967/month.

localEntities.name: Belknap/Merrimack (Concord: 603-223-0043)
Rockingham (Portsmouth: 603-436-3896)
Strafford (Dover: 603-435-2500)
Other regions via local Community Action Partnership of NH (CAPNH)

programs.intro:
If your loved one struggles to pay heating bills on a fixed income, LIHEAP can provide $94 to $2,177 toward their annual heating costs. Because this program pays vendors directly, your loved one never handles the money — payments go straight to their oil, gas, or electric company. The program prioritizes seniors, disabled adults, and households with young children under 6.

Income limits are higher than many programs: $3,967/month for a single person, $5,187 for two people (2025). Because LIHEAP includes everyone at the address on the same utility bill as one household, roommate situations can affect eligibility even if your loved one doesn't share expenses with them.

Applications open July 30, 2025 for the heating season, but priority groups (elderly and disabled) can submit pre-applications starting July 1. Because funding runs out each year, apply as early as possible through your regional Community Action Agency.
programs.savingsSource: Direct heating assistance payments to energy vendors

otherRequirements: Must reside in New Hampshire
Both renters and homeowners eligible
Everyone at the address counts as household if on same utility bill

applicationGuide.summary: Call your regional Community Action Agency to schedule an intake appointment — processing time varies but funds are limited each season.

steps.title: Find your regional Community Action Agency
steps.description: Belknap/Merrimack counties call 603-223-0043, Rockingham County call 603-436-3896, Strafford County call 603-435-2500. Other regions contact Community Action Partnership of NH.

steps.description: If your loved one is elderly, disabled, or has young children under 6, submit pre-application starting July 1, 2025. All others can start September 1, 2025.

steps.title: Schedule intake appointment
steps.description: Call for phone appointment when applications open July 30, 2025. Offices are open Monday-Friday 8:30am-4:30pm year-round.

steps.title: Complete application interview
steps.description: Provide all required documents during your appointment. The agency will review income, household size, and heating needs.

steps.title: Receive vendor payment
steps.description: If approved, payments go directly to your loved one's heating vendor — oil company, gas utility, or electric company.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified — apply early as funds are limited each season
applicationGuide.tip: Apply immediately when your priority window opens — funding is distributed first-come, first-served until depleted each heating season.

applicationNotes: Priority households (elderly, disabled, families with children under 6) can submit pre-applications starting July 1, while others wait until September 1
Everyone living at your loved one's address counts as the household if they're on the same utility bill, even if they don't share expenses
Emergency crisis assistance is available year-round for heating emergencies like shutoffs or broken furnaces, up to $2,177
Payments never go to your loved one — all assistance is paid directly to the heating vendor or utility company

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply if they have a roommate who isn't related to them?
faqs.answer: Yes, but everyone at the address counts as one household if they're on the same utility bill, even if they don't share expenses. This affects the income calculation and household size determination. The total household income cannot exceed the limit for the total number of people living there.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's heating company won't accept LIHEAP payments?
faqs.answer: Most heating vendors in New Hampshire accept LIHEAP payments, but if yours doesn't, contact your Community Action Agency immediately. They can help resolve vendor issues or find alternative solutions. Don't let this delay your application — apply first and resolve vendor problems during processing.

faqs.question: Can my loved one get both regular heating assistance and emergency crisis help in the same year?
faqs.answer: Yes, if your loved one qualifies for regular seasonal heating assistance, they can still receive emergency crisis assistance up to $2,177 if they face a heating emergency like a shutoff or broken furnace. Crisis assistance is available year-round, not just during heating season.

faqs.question: How early should we apply when the season opens?
faqs.answer: Apply immediately when your window opens — priority groups on July 1 for pre-applications, others September 1, with full applications starting July 30, 2025. Funding is distributed first-come, first-served until depleted. Many agencies run out of funds before winter ends.

faqs.question: Does LIHEAP assistance count as income for other programs like Medicaid or SNAP?
faqs.answer: No, LIHEAP payments do not count as income for other federal benefit programs. Because payments go directly to heating vendors, your loved one never receives cash that could affect other program eligibility.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one moves to a different county in New Hampshire during the heating season?
faqs.answer: Contact the Community Action Agency in your loved one's new county immediately. They'll need to transfer the case or reapply through the new regional agency. Don't assume coverage automatically transfers — each county has its own Community Action Agency with separate funding.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one who has dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can apply on behalf of elderly or disabled parents who cannot manage the application themselves. You'll need documentation of your authority to act on their behalf, such as power of attorney papers. Discuss this with the intake worker when you call to schedule the appointment.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's income varies month to month because of part-time work or irregular Social Security?
faqs.answer: Provide documentation for the most recent 30 days of income as requested. If income varies significantly, the agency may ask for additional months to calculate an average. Be prepared to explain any unusual income fluctuations during your intake appointment.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program (WAP)
programs.tagline: Free energy-saving home improvements that can cut your loved one's heating bills by hundreds of dollars each year.

localEntities.name: CAPBM: Merrimack/Belknap Counties
CAPHR: Hillsborough/Rockingham Counties
CAPSC: Strafford County

programs.intro:
If your loved one struggles with high heating bills and qualifies for fuel assistance, they may be eligible for free home weatherization services. Because the Weatherization Assistance Program covers all energy-saving improvements at no cost, your loved one could see significant reductions in monthly utility bills without any upfront investment.

The program provides a comprehensive energy audit followed by improvements like air sealing, insulation, LED bulbs, low-flow devices, and potentially even heating system repairs or refrigerator replacement. Because services are determined by what will be most cost-effective for your loved one's specific home, the improvements are tailored to deliver maximum energy savings.

You must first qualify through New Hampshire's Fuel Assistance Program (FAP) or Electric Assistance Program (EAP) — typically at or below 200% of Federal Poverty Guidelines. Priority goes to elderly households, disabled individuals, families with young children, and those with high energy burdens.

summary: Must qualify for NH Fuel Assistance or Electric Assistance
Income typically at or below 200% Federal Poverty Guidelines
Home cannot have major structural issues
Priority for elderly and disabled households
otherRequirements: Must qualify for and/or participate in Fuel Assistance Program (FAP) or Electric Assistance Program (EAP)
Home cannot have significant structural issues, disrepair, leaking roof, or failing plumbing/electrical systems
Homeowners and renters eligible (renters need landlord permission)
Homes weatherized after September 1994 generally ineligible
Multi-unit buildings require at least 66% of units enrolled in fuel/electric assistance

faqs.question: What if my loved one's landlord refuses to give permission?
faqs.answer: Renters must have landlord permission to receive weatherization services. Try explaining that the improvements increase property value at no cost to the landlord. If they still refuse, your loved one cannot participate in the program.

faqs.question: Can we apply for weatherization if our home was improved years ago?
faqs.answer: Homes weatherized after September 1994 are generally ineligible for new services. However, homes weatherized before that date may qualify for updated improvements on a case-by-case basis.

faqs.question: What happens if the energy audit finds our home needs major repairs first?
faqs.answer: The program cannot proceed if your home has significant structural issues, leaking roof, or failing plumbing/electrical systems. These must be fixed at your own expense before weatherization services can begin.

faqs.question: How much will this actually save on heating bills?
faqs.answer: Savings vary by home size, condition, and current energy efficiency. The energy audit will provide specific projections for your loved one's home based on recommended improvements.

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply for both fuel assistance and weatherization at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes — in fact, you must apply for fuel assistance first. Answer 'YES' to weatherization interest on the fuel assistance application to be considered for both programs.

faqs.question: What if we live in an apartment building?
faqs.answer: Multi-unit buildings can participate if at least 66% of units are enrolled in fuel assistance or electric assistance programs. Contact your local Community Action Partnership to discuss building-wide eligibility.

programs.name: SHIP (State Health Insurance Assistance Program)
programs.tagline: Free one-on-one help understanding Medicare options, comparing plans, and avoiding overpayment.

localEntities.name: Administered statewide by ServiceLink Resource Center (NH DHHS Bureau of Elderly & Adult Services in Concord); local access via phone/website statewide

programs.intro:
If your loved one has Medicare and you're confused about their coverage options, SHIP provides free one-on-one counseling to help you understand benefits, compare plans, and avoid overpaying. Because SHIP counselors are trained volunteers with no financial stake in your decisions, they can give unbiased advice about Medicare Part A/B, Part D prescription coverage, Medigap supplements, and Medicare Advantage plans.

SHIP is available to all Medicare beneficiaries and their families regardless of income. The service includes help with Medicare Savings Programs that could lower your loved one's costs, prescription assistance programs, and referrals to other benefits they might qualify for. Counseling is confidential and no personal information is shared unless you request it.

otherRequirements: Must be a Medicare beneficiary or family member/caregiver with questions about Medicare or related insurance

applicationGuide.summary: Call 1-866-634-9412 for immediate counseling during business hours or to schedule an appointment.

steps.title: Call the SHIP helpline
steps.description: Call 1-866-634-9412, Monday through Friday 8am-4pm. No appointment needed during these hours for basic questions.

steps.title: Schedule detailed counseling if needed
steps.description: For complex situations like plan comparisons or enrollment help, schedule a longer phone appointment or visit ServiceLink Resource Center at 105 Pleasant St., Concord.

steps.title: Gather Medicare information
steps.description: Have your loved one's Medicare card and current insurance information ready if discussing specific enrollment or plan changes.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Immediate counseling available by phone during business hours
applicationGuide.tip: Call during open enrollment periods (October 15 - December 7) early in the morning for faster service.

contacts.label: SHIP Counseling Line
contacts.description: Free Medicare counseling and plan comparison help

contacts.label: ServiceLink Resource Center
contacts.description: In-person appointments and comprehensive benefits counseling

faqs.question: Can SHIP help me enroll my loved one in a Medicare plan?
faqs.answer: SHIP counselors explain your options and guide you through the process, but they don't handle enrollments directly. They'll help you compare plans and refer you to the right place to enroll, whether that's Medicare.gov, the insurance company, or another agency.

faqs.question: My loved one has both Medicare and Medicaid. Can SHIP still help?
faqs.answer: Yes, SHIP counselors understand dual eligibility and can explain how Medicare and Medicaid work together. They can also help you understand Special Needs Plans (SNPs) designed specifically for people with both programs.

faqs.question: What's the difference between SHIP and the Medicare Savings Programs they mention?
faqs.answer: SHIP is free counseling available to everyone with Medicare questions. Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI) are separate financial assistance programs with income limits up to $1,660 for singles that help pay Medicare premiums and costs. SHIP counselors can explain these programs and help you apply.

programs.name: Meals on Wheels (Elderly Nutrition Program)
programs.tagline: Home-delivered meals for parents 60+ who have difficulty shopping or preparing food.

localEntities.name: Rockingham Nutrition & Meals on Wheels (Rockingham County)

Coalition via Meals on Wheels NH (https://mealsonwheelsnh.org)

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 60+ and struggles to shop for groceries or prepare meals, Meals on Wheels delivers nutritionally balanced meals directly to their home — typically one meal per weekday (5 meals per week). The meals are certified by dietitians and designed to help your loved one maintain independence while getting proper nutrition.

Unlike many programs, there are no income limits for adults 60+. Your loved one only needs to be homebound or have difficulty leaving home safely, shopping, or preparing meals. Because each county operates its own program through local nonprofits, you must confirm your loved one lives within a specific provider's delivery zone.

The biggest gotcha: this program is not statewide. If your loved one lives outside a provider's service area — even by a few miles — they cannot receive meals. Some areas also have waitlists that can extend weeks or months depending on demand.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be homebound or have difficulty leaving home safely, shopping for groceries, or preparing nutritionally adequate meals
otherRequirements: Must live within the specific delivery zone of a local Meals on Wheels provider
Must be unable to meet basic nutritional needs, either temporarily or long-term

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local provider directly through the Meals on Wheels NH website or call their regional number — processing typically takes within a week if no waitlist.

steps.title: Complete intake assessment

steps.title: Schedule meal delivery
steps.description: Once approved, the provider will coordinate delivery times and any special dietary accommodations. Meals typically arrive on weekdays during morning or lunch hours.

faqs.question: Can my loved one get meals if they live just outside the official service area?
faqs.answer: No. Each provider has strict geographic boundaries, often by county or specific towns. Even living a few miles outside the zone means no service. Check the exact address with the local provider before applying — this is the most common reason people can't access the program.

faqs.question: What if my loved one can still drive but has trouble cooking?
faqs.answer: This depends on the local provider's assessment. Some accept difficulty with meal preparation alone, while others require true homebound status. Be honest about your loved one's specific limitations during the intake assessment — many providers also offer congregate dining or grab-and-go options for people who can still leave home.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist really?
faqs.answer: It varies dramatically by region and season. Some providers can start service within a week, while others have waitlists lasting months. Winter months typically have longer waits due to higher demand. Ask about current wait times when you call, and consider asking to be notified if someone cancels service.

faqs.question: Can I apply for my loved one if they have dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can apply on behalf of parents who cannot manage the process themselves. You'll need to provide emergency contacts and may need to be present during the assessment. Some providers also coordinate with healthcare teams for clients with cognitive impairments.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one needs special dietary meals for diabetes or heart conditions?
faqs.answer: Most providers accommodate dietary restrictions and medical needs. During the assessment, provide detailed information about prescribed diets, food allergies, and texture modifications. Meals are certified by dietitians, but availability of specialized meals varies by provider.

faqs.question: Can my loved one get meals temporarily while recovering from surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes, many providers offer temporary service for people recovering from illness, surgery, or medical procedures. These short-term cases are often prioritized over long-term applicants. Hospital discharge planners sometimes help initiate the process before your loved one comes home.

faqs.question: What if there's no Meals on Wheels in our area of New Hampshire?
faqs.answer: Some rural areas lack coverage. Contact New Hampshire 2-1-1 to ask about alternative meal programs, senior centers with congregate dining, or food delivery services that accept benefit payments. Your loved one's Area Agency on Aging may also know about smaller local programs not listed on statewide directories.

faqs.question: Does my loved one's income affect eligibility if they're over 60?
faqs.answer: No income limits apply to adults 60+. The program prioritizes functional need (difficulty shopping or cooking) over financial need for seniors. Income limits only apply to adults 18-59 with disabilities who want to access the program early.

programs.name: Family Caregiver Support Program
programs.tagline: Free respite care, counseling, and support services for family members caring for aging loved ones or relatives with disabilities.

localEntities.name: Aging and Disability Resource Centers (ADRCs)
Granite State Independent Living

programs.intro:
If you're caring for an aging loved one 60+ or a relative with Alzheimer's, you can get free respite care, counseling, and support services through New Hampshire's Family Caregiver Support Program. The program provides temporary breaks from caregiving, support groups, caregiver training, and limited help with home modifications, transportation, and assistive equipment.

No income limits apply — services are available to all family caregivers regardless of financial situation. Because this program is funded through the Older Americans Act, you access services through regional Aging and Disability Resource Centers statewide rather than applying for benefits directly.

The program serves different caregiver types: adult children caring for parents 60+, caregivers of people with dementia (must live together), and grandparents 55+ raising grandchildren or caring for disabled adult relatives.

summary: Caring for parent 60+ or person with dementia
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Care recipient must need assistance with at least two Activities of Daily Living (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, walking, or getting in/out of bed)
otherRequirements: Must be family member or informal caregiver (not paid professional)
For dementia caregivers: must live under same roof with care recipient
For grandparents raising children: child must live full-time with grandparent in absence of parent

steps.title: Schedule an assessment

steps.title: Begin receiving services
steps.description: Services like respite care, support groups, and caregiver training can often begin within days of your assessment, though some services may have waiting periods.

applicationGuide.tip: Have details ready about your relative's daily care needs and what type of help would be most useful to you as a caregiver.

faqs.question: Can I get help if my loved one doesn't have dementia but needs daily care?
faqs.answer: Yes, if your loved one is 60+ and needs help with at least two activities of daily living (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, walking, or getting in/out of bed). The program serves all older adult caregiving situations, not just dementia. Call 866-634-9412 to discuss your specific situation.

faqs.question: What's the difference between respite care and regular home care?
faqs.answer: Respite care through this program is temporary relief for you as the caregiver — someone comes to stay with your loved one while you take a break, run errands, or attend appointments. It's not ongoing daily care, but rather scheduled breaks to prevent caregiver burnout.

faqs.question: Can I get financial help paying a family member to provide care?
faqs.answer: No, this program does not provide direct payments to caregivers. It focuses on support services like respite care, counseling, and training. If you need paid family caregiving, ask about Medicaid programs like Consumer Directed Family Care when you call.

faqs.question: What if my loved one lives in assisted living — can I still get caregiver support?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members providing care to relatives in assisted living can still access support groups, counseling, and caregiver training through this program. However, respite care may not be needed if professional care is already in place.

faqs.question: How much respite care can I get each month?
faqs.answer: The amount of respite care varies based on your needs assessment and local provider capacity. Some caregivers receive a few hours weekly, others may get occasional full days. Your local ADRC will work with you to determine appropriate levels of support.

faqs.question: Can I access services immediately if I'm in a caregiving crisis?
faqs.answer: Contact your local ADRC immediately for crisis situations. While some services like support groups may be available quickly, respite care providers may need time to arrange. Having a crisis plan in place before you need it is ideal.

faqs.question: What happens if my caregiving situation changes?
faqs.answer: Services can be adjusted as your needs change. If your loved one's condition worsens or improves, or if your own circumstances change, contact your ADRC to reassess and modify your service plan accordingly.

faqs.question: Can I use this program while also receiving other aging services?
faqs.answer: Yes, caregiver support services can complement other programs your loved one may be receiving like Meals on Wheels, senior transportation, or medical services. The ADRC can help coordinate multiple services to avoid duplication.

programs.name: SCSEP (Senior Community Service Employment Program)
programs.tagline: Part-time community service work for unemployed adults 55+ at minimum wage while building job skills.

localEntities.name: Operation ABLE

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 55+ and unemployed with limited income, SCSEP provides part-time paid work (average 20 hours per week) at community organizations like schools, hospitals, and senior centers. Participants earn at least minimum wage while gaining job training and support to find permanent employment.

Because SCSEP prioritizes certain groups first — veterans, adults 65+, disabled individuals, and those with limited English or low literacy — your loved one may face a waitlist depending on their situation. Income limit: no more than 125% of federal poverty level (varies by household size and updates annually).

The program is temporary training, not permanent employment. The goal is to help participants transition to unsubsidized jobs in the community.

summary: Income at 125% poverty level or below
otherRequirements: Must be unemployed at time of enrollment
Must be eligible to work in the United States
Must want to learn or refresh job skills

applicationGuide.summary: Call Operation ABLE, New Hampshire's SCSEP provider, or the national hotline 1-877-872-5627.

steps.title: Complete enrollment process
steps.description: Work with the provider to complete application paperwork and income verification. Priority enrollment goes to veterans, adults 65+, disabled individuals, and other specified groups.

steps.title: Job placement assignment
steps.description: If accepted, you'll be matched with a community service position at a local non-profit or public agency based on your skills and interests.

applicationGuide.tip: Veterans and their qualified spouses get first priority, followed by adults 65+, so mention veteran status if applicable.

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply if they're collecting Social Security or pension benefits?
faqs.answer: Yes, as long as their total household income stays at or below 125% of federal poverty level. Social Security and pension income count toward the income limit, but the program allows participants to work part-time while receiving these benefits.

faqs.question: How long can my loved one stay in the SCSEP program?
faqs.answer: SCSEP is designed as temporary training, not permanent employment. The goal is to help participants gain skills and transition to unsubsidized jobs in the community. Specific time limits vary by individual circumstances and local provider policies.

faqs.question: What if my loved one doesn't fall into any priority groups — how long is the waitlist?
faqs.answer: Waitlist times vary by local demand and aren't specified in program guidelines. Priority goes first to veterans/spouses, then adults 65+, disabled individuals, those with limited English, low literacy, rural residents, homeless individuals, and those with poor employment prospects.

faqs.question: Can my loved one choose what type of work they do in the program?
faqs.answer: Participants are matched with community service positions based on their skills, interests, and available host sites. Common placements include schools, hospitals, day-care centers, and senior centers, but specific assignments depend on local opportunities.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one finds a regular job while in SCSEP?
faqs.answer: That's the program's goal. SCSEP includes job search assistance and is designed to help participants transition to permanent unsubsidized employment. Finding regular work is considered a successful program completion.

faqs.question: Does my loved one need specific job skills or education to qualify?
faqs.answer: No specific skills or education requirements are listed. The program is designed for people who want to learn or refresh job skills, regardless of their current skill level or educational background.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Contact Operation ABLE or call 1-877-872-5627 to discuss application procedures for elderly applicants who may need assistance. Program providers can explain what documentation and authorization might be needed for family members to help with the process.

faqs.question: Will SCSEP work affect my loved one's other benefits like Medicaid or SNAP?
faqs.answer: SCSEP earnings count as income for other benefit programs. Because participants earn at least minimum wage for part-time work, this could affect eligibility for income-based programs. Contact each benefit program directly to understand how SCSEP income will impact existing benefits.

programs.name: Legal Assistance for Older Adults
programs.tagline: Free legal help for seniors 60+ with housing, benefits appeals, estate planning, and consumer protection.

localEntities.name: Primary office: Concord, NH; statewide centralized intake via 603 Legal Aid/NHLA

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 60+ and facing legal issues like landlord disputes, Medicare appeals, debt collection, or needs help with wills and power of attorney, New Hampshire provides free civil legal services specifically for seniors. Because this program covers everything from housing problems to healthcare appeals under one umbrella, your loved one won't need to find separate lawyers for different issues.

The program prioritizes low-income and isolated seniors but serves all New Hampshire residents age 60+ regardless of income or assets. Services include court representation, advice calls, negotiation with creditors or landlords, and help navigating government benefits appeals.

otherRequirements: New Hampshire resident
Civil legal issues (housing, benefits, family law, consumer issues, estate planning)

faqs.question: Can my loved one get help even if they have savings or own their home?
faqs.answer: Yes. The program serves all New Hampshire residents age 60+ regardless of income or assets. Low-income and isolated seniors get priority, but services are available to everyone who qualifies by age and has civil legal issues.

faqs.question: What types of legal problems can they help with?
faqs.answer: Civil legal issues only — housing disputes, landlord problems, consumer contracts, debt collection, utility shutoffs, healthcare appeals, Medicare/Medicaid issues, family law, estate planning, wills, and power of attorney. They cannot help with criminal cases.

faqs.question: Can I call on behalf of my loved one?
faqs.answer: Contact the intake line to discuss your specific situation. Generally, legal aid programs need to speak directly with the client, but they can guide you on how to help your loved one access services if they have cognitive or communication challenges.

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.tagline: Free advocate to resolve complaints and protect your loved one's rights in nursing homes and assisted living facilities.

localEntities.name: Central office: 129 Pleasant Street, Concord, NH 03301-3857

programs.intro:
If your loved one lives in a New Hampshire nursing home, assisted living facility, or other long-term care facility, they have a free advocate available when problems arise. The Long-Term Care Ombudsman investigates complaints about care quality, helps resolve conflicts with facility staff, and ensures your loved one's rights are protected — all at no cost regardless of income.

This program covers all types of long-term care facilities in New Hampshire, including nursing homes, assisted living, adult day care, long-term rehabilitation centers, and hospice facilities. Because ombudsmen work independently from facilities and government agencies, they can advocate solely for your loved one's interests without conflicts of interest.

Services are completely confidential and available to residents, family members, and facility staff. The program focuses on advocacy and complaint resolution — they don't provide direct medical care or financial assistance, but they can help ensure your loved one receives the care and respect they deserve.

summary: Resident of NH long-term care facility
otherRequirements: Must be a resident of a long-term care facility in New Hampshire, or family/staff acting on behalf of a resident

applicationGuide.summary: Call the toll-free number to discuss your concerns — no forms or application process required.

steps.title: Discuss your situation confidentially
steps.description: An ombudsman will listen to your concerns and explain your options. Services are confidential and you can remain anonymous if preferred.

steps.title: Ombudsman investigates and advocates
steps.description: The ombudsman will work with the facility, your loved one, and family members to resolve the issue and ensure proper care standards are met.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Services provided as needed upon contact — no waiting period for complaint resolution to begin
applicationGuide.tip: You can also fax concerns to (603) 271-5574 or visit in person at 129 Pleasant Street in Concord if phone contact isn't convenient.

faqs.question: Can I contact the ombudsman without my loved one knowing?
faqs.answer: Yes, services are confidential and you can remain anonymous if needed. However, the ombudsman will likely need to speak with your loved one at some point to fully understand the situation and advocate effectively. They will discuss the best approach with you first.

faqs.question: What if the facility retaliates against my loved one for filing a complaint?
faqs.answer: Retaliation is illegal and the ombudsman is specifically trained to prevent and address this. They monitor the situation after complaints are filed and can take additional action if any form of retaliation occurs. Call (800) 442-5640 immediately if you suspect retaliation.

faqs.question: Does the ombudsman help with billing disputes or insurance problems?
faqs.answer: The ombudsman focuses on care quality and resident rights, not financial disputes with facilities or insurance companies. For billing issues, contact NH Legal Aid or your loved one's insurance company directly. The ombudsman can help if poor care quality is related to payment disputes.

faqs.question: Can the ombudsman help us choose a good facility before my loved one moves in?
faqs.answer: Yes, ombudsmen can provide information about facilities and help you understand what questions to ask during visits. Call (800) 442-5640 to discuss your loved one's needs and get guidance on evaluating potential facilities.

programs.name: Adult Day Services
programs.tagline: Medical day care with nursing supervision to help your loved one stay home longer while you work.

localEntities.name: Licensed under He-P 818; examples include facilities like Maple Leaf in southern NH, Rockingham County programs

programs.intro:
If your loved one needs daily medical supervision but doesn't require 24-hour nursing home care, Adult Day Services provides structured medical day care with registered nurses on-site. Your loved one gets health monitoring, medication management, therapeutic activities, and meals for at least 4 hours per day while living at home.

This is a Medicaid-covered service for New Hampshire residents who are at risk of immediate institutionalization without support. Because all services are provided under medical supervision with care plans reviewed every 90 days, your loved one receives coordinated health care rather than fragmented services from multiple providers.

To qualify, your loved one must be Medicaid-eligible with income below $2,901/month and assets under $2,500 for a single person. They need a physician referral and physical exam within 60 days of starting services.

faqs.question: Can my loved one keep their house if it's worth more than the $2,500 asset limit?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is exempt from asset limits if your loved one intends to return home. This exemption applies regardless of the home's value as long as it remains their primary residence.

faqs.question: What happens if the adult day provider says they can't meet my loved one's needs?
faqs.answer: Providers can legally deny admission if your loved one's care needs exceed their capacity or create safety risks. You'll need to find another licensed provider or explore different care options like home health services or nursing home care.

faqs.question: Can I apply for adult day services and nursing home Medicaid at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can apply for both since they use the same Medicaid eligibility criteria. This gives you backup options if adult day services become insufficient or unavailable.

faqs.question: How often does my loved one need to see their doctor for this program?
faqs.answer: The initial physician exam and referral must be within 60 days of starting services. After that, the care plan is reviewed every 90 days, which may require physician input depending on your loved one's condition.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's income is too high for regular Medicaid but they need these services?
faqs.answer: New Hampshire has a Medicaid spend-down program where you can deduct medical expenses to meet the income limit of $2,901/month. Contact DHHS to see if your loved one's medical costs qualify for spend-down.

faqs.question: Can my loved one attend adult day services just one day per week?
faqs.answer: Yes, the minimum requirement is at least 4 hours per day on a regularly scheduled basis, which can be as little as one day per week. The schedule depends on your loved one's care plan and the provider's availability.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's condition gets worse while in adult day services?
faqs.answer: The care plan is reviewed every 90 days and can be modified if needs change. However, if your loved one requires more care than the provider can safely deliver, they may need to transition to a higher level of care like nursing home placement.

faqs.question: If my loved one has dementia, does that disqualify them from adult day services?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Dementia with medical complications qualifies, but primary mental illness or developmental disabilities are excluded. The provider will assess whether they can safely manage your loved one's specific combination of conditions.

programs.name: Easterseals NH In-Home Care & Health Services
programs.tagline: Professional in-home nursing, personal care, and homemaker services when your loved one needs help staying at home safely.

localEntities.name: Offices in Manchester and Keene for related services, but in-home care via statewide team

programs.intro:
If your loved one needs help with daily tasks like bathing, medication management, or light housekeeping but wants to stay at home, Easterseals NH provides professional in-home care services statewide. They offer nursing services (medication management and health monitoring), personal care assistance (bathing and daily routines), and homemaker services (meal preparation, errands, light cleaning).

Unlike government waiver programs with strict income limits, Easterseals NH accepts multiple payment methods including Medicaid, long-term care insurance, Veterans vouchers, and private pay. Because they create personalized care plans after a home visit, services are tailored to your loved one's specific needs rather than following a one-size-fits-all approach.

Services are available statewide but depend on staff availability in your area. The program focuses primarily on the Seacoast, Greater Nashua, Greater Manchester, and Greater Rochester regions, though their Caring Companions non-medical program serves the entire state.

faqs.question: What's the difference between this and a state Medicaid waiver program?
faqs.answer: Easterseals NH is a private provider that accepts various payment methods, including Medicaid, insurance, and private pay. Unlike state waiver programs with strict income limits and long waitlists, you can start services once staff availability allows, regardless of your loved one's income if paying privately or through insurance.

faqs.question: How much will this cost if my loved one doesn't qualify for Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Costs vary based on services needed and payment method. The program accepts long-term care insurance, Veterans vouchers, and private pay. You'll receive specific pricing during the home visit assessment based on your loved one's personalized care plan.

faqs.question: What if there's no staff available in my loved one's area?
faqs.answer: Services depend on staff availability and are concentrated in Seacoast, Greater Nashua, Greater Manchester, and Greater Rochester regions. If nursing or personal care isn't available, ask about the statewide Caring Companions program for non-medical support.

faqs.question: Can I get services started while waiting for Medicaid approval?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can begin services with private pay or insurance while Medicaid processes, then switch payment methods once approved. Discuss this transition plan during your initial consultation at 603-845-9318.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's care needs change after we start services?
faqs.answer: Care plans are personalized and can be adjusted as needs change. Contact your care coordinator to reassess services, modify schedules, or add new types of support like transitioning from homemaker services to personal care.

faqs.question: How is this different from hiring a private aide on my own?
faqs.answer: Easterseals NH provides trained, supervised staff with backup coverage, handles payroll and insurance, and offers nursing oversight for medical needs. They also coordinate with other healthcare providers and create comprehensive care plans rather than just providing individual tasks.

programs.name: Caregiver Respite and Senior Volunteer Programs
programs.tagline: Get a break from caregiving with in-home help, adult day programs, or short-term stays for your loved one.

localEntities.name: Monadnock Region and Sullivan County Aging and Disability Resource Centers

programs.intro:
If you're caring for an aging loved one and need a break, New Hampshire offers respite grants and volunteer programs to give you temporary relief. These services include in-home caregivers hired by agencies for personal care and companionship, adult day programs, and emergency respite when you face an unexpected crisis. Priority goes to caregivers of adults 55+ with severe disabilities.

The program operates regionally through local Aging and Disability Resource Centers, with different services available in different parts of the state. Because availability varies by location and beds are limited, you may not get your first choice of provider, but counselors work to find options that fit your family's needs. Some services are funded through state grants, while others are private pay arrangements.

faqs.question: What's the difference between the free respite grants and private services?
faqs.answer: State-funded grants through Aging and Disability Resource Centers provide services at no cost, including in-home respite and adult day programs. Private providers like Comfort Keepers and St. Teresa require payment but may offer more flexible scheduling and immediate availability. Your options counselor can help you understand what's available for free in your area.

faqs.question: Can I get emergency respite if I have a health crisis or family emergency?
faqs.answer: Yes, emergency respite is available but limited to 10% of each region's budget. This means availability may be restricted, so contact your Aging and Disability Resource Center as soon as possible when a crisis occurs. Regular respite planning is recommended to avoid relying solely on emergency services.

faqs.question: What if there are no services available in my part of New Hampshire?
faqs.answer: The program operates regionally with different services in different areas. If your local Aging and Disability Resource Center doesn't have grant-funded options available, they can refer you to private providers or help you access statewide programs. Call ServiceLink at 1-866-634-9412 for alternatives if your regional center can't help.

faqs.question: How long can respite care last - a few hours or several days?
faqs.answer: Both options are available depending on the provider and your needs. In-home respite can range from a few hours per week to 24/7 care. Facilities like St. Teresa offer short-term stays of 3+ days. Adult day programs typically provide daytime care. Your needs assessment will help determine what duration works best for your situation.

faqs.question: Can I choose which caregiver comes to my loved one's home?
faqs.answer: For grant-funded services, the agency hires and assigns caregivers, though you can request specific qualifications or express preferences. Private providers may offer more choice in caregiver selection. All in-home respite workers provide personal care, homemaking, and companionship based on your loved one's assessed needs.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one doesn't qualify for priority services?
faqs.answer: Priority goes to caregivers of adults 55+ with severe disabilities, but all unpaid family caregivers are encouraged to explore options. Even if you don't get priority, you may still access services based on availability. The assessment process will identify what programs might work for your family's specific situation.

programs.name: AmeriCorps Senior Companion Program (NH)
programs.tagline: Your loved one can earn $3/hour helping homebound adults while gaining insurance coverage and meaningful work

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 55+ and earns less than $25,760/year (single) or $34,840/year (couple), they can join New Hampshire's AmeriCorps Senior Companion Program. Because this combines volunteer work with income support, your loved one earns $3/hour tax-free while providing companionship to homebound adults — plus mileage reimbursement, accident insurance, and paid holidays.

Your loved one would visit isolated seniors Monday through Friday, 8:30am–4:30pm, providing conversation and friendship (not housekeeping or personal care). Because companions must commit to at least 10 hours per week, this works best for parents who want structured social interaction and modest supplemental income. The program operates in 7 New Hampshire counties with 70 current volunteers statewide.

faqs.question: Will the $3/hour stipend affect my loved one's Social Security or other benefits?
faqs.answer: The stipend is tax-free and non-reportable, so it should not affect Social Security, SNAP, or housing assistance. However, confirm this with your benefits administrator before enrolling, especially if your loved one receives SSI or Medicaid with strict income limits.

faqs.question: What if my loved one doesn't feel comfortable driving clients in their car?
faqs.answer: Transportation of clients is required, and companions must have their own reliable vehicle with required auto insurance coverage. If your loved one cannot drive clients safely, this program may not be suitable — contact Kathleen Stuart at (603) 225-3295 to discuss alternatives.

faqs.question: Can my loved one choose which days and hours to volunteer?
faqs.answer: Visits are only available Monday through Friday, 8:30am–4:30pm, with a minimum 10-hour weekly commitment. The Program Coordinator will work with your loved one to arrange a schedule that fits within these parameters and matches them with compatible clients.

faqs.question: What counties does this program cover in New Hampshire?
faqs.answer: The program operates in Belknap, Grafton, Hillsborough, Merrimack, Rockingham, Strafford, and Sullivan counties. Contact (603) 225-3295 to confirm availability in your loved one's specific area and current volunteer openings.

programs.name: Monadnock at Home Community Services
programs.tagline: Comprehensive support network helping seniors in 12 Monadnock Region towns maintain independence at home

localEntities.name: Program of Catholic Charities NH, serving Monadnock Region towns.

programs.intro:
If your loved one lives in Dublin, Fitzwilliam, Greenfield, Hancock, Harrisville, Jaffrey, Marlborough, New Ipswich, Peterborough, Rindge, Sharon, or Temple, they can access a comprehensive support network designed to help them stay in their home safely. Monadnock at Home provides one phone number for multiple services: volunteers for basic home maintenance, vetted home health aides, grocery and medication delivery, transportation to medical appointments, and regular check-in calls.

The program operates as a membership-based service with a nominal fee, connecting seniors to pre-screened local providers and volunteers. Because all services coordinate through one central contact, your loved one won't need to manage multiple provider relationships or wonder who to call when they need help with everything from changing a lightbulb to finding reliable pet care.

The service area is limited to 12 towns in the Monadnock Region, and the program partners with local entities like Monadnock Community Hospital to ensure comprehensive care coordination.

faqs.answer: The program requires a 'nominal fee' but the exact amount is not published online. Contact Catholic Charities NH through their website form to get current pricing and payment options. The fee is not income-tested, so all members pay the same amount regardless of financial situation.

faqs.question: What if my loved one lives in Keene or another nearby town not on the list?
faqs.answer: Monadnock at Home only serves 12 specific towns east of Mount Monadnock. If your loved one lives outside these towns, they won't be eligible for this program. Contact NH ServiceLink at 1-866-634-9412 to find similar aging-in-place services in other areas.

faqs.question: Can I sign up my loved one if they have dementia or can't make their own decisions?
faqs.answer: The program doesn't specify cognitive requirements for enrollment. Contact Catholic Charities NH to discuss your loved one's situation and whether family members can enroll on behalf of someone with cognitive impairment. They may have specific protocols for working with power of attorney or guardianship situations.

faqs.question: How quickly can my loved one get help after enrolling?
faqs.answer: The program doesn't publish response times for different services. Volunteer availability for basic tasks like home maintenance may vary seasonally, while vetted provider services likely have their own scheduling. Ask about typical response times for the specific services your loved one needs most when you enroll.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one needs more help than this program provides?
faqs.answer: Monadnock at Home focuses on helping seniors maintain independence, not providing intensive care. If your loved one's needs increase beyond what volunteers and vetted providers can handle, the program can likely help connect you to home health agencies, assisted living facilities, or other care options through their network.

faqs.question: Does this program work with Medicare or other insurance?
faqs.answer: As a membership-based service network, Monadnock at Home operates separately from insurance programs. However, some of the vetted providers they connect you to (like home health aides) may accept Medicare or private insurance for their services. Ask about insurance coordination when requesting specific provider referrals.

stateOverview.intro:
New Hampshire offers 17 programs to help your aging loved one — 13 direct benefits that require qualification and 4 free resources available to everyone. These range from monthly grocery assistance ($100-400+ through SNAP) and heating bill help ($94-2,177 through LIHEAP) to comprehensive medical care under one team (NH PACE) and free in-home respite so you can take a break from caregiving. If your loved one is 60+ on a fixed income or needs help with daily activities, multiple programs likely apply — and you can combine them.

Most income-based programs use similar thresholds: around $1,660/month for Medicare savings programs, $3,967/month for heating assistance, with higher limits for programs like Medicaid long-term care. Start with the biggest financial impacts first, then layer in free resources for ongoing support.

startHere.name: Medicaid for Aged, Blind, Disabled (ABD)
startHere.why: Covers long-term care costs that Medicare doesn't, potentially saving tens of thousands in nursing home or home care expenses.

startHere.why: Provides immediate monthly grocery assistance of $100-400+ for seniors on fixed incomes.

quickFacts: New Hampshire has no statewide online portal — most benefit applications require calling individual programs or visiting local offices.
The Monadnock at Home program serves only 12 specific towns in the Monadnock Region, while other programs are statewide.
Income limits vary widely: $1,660/month for Medicare savings programs, $3,967/month for heating assistance, with asset limits typically under $4,000 for individuals.
stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: Benefits like SNAP, LIHEAP, and Medicaid require your loved one to meet specific income and asset limits — but the payoff can be hundreds or thousands in monthly assistance. Resources like SHIP counseling, legal aid, and ombudsman services are free to all New Hampshire seniors regardless of income, making them your first stop for advice before applying to qualification-based programs.
